source_text: Sender's Symbols. Include the following three identification symbols in the upper left corner, blocked one below the other: (1) SSIC; (2) Originator's Code, by itself or in a Serial Number; and (3) Date. The originator's code, by itself or without the serial number, is the originator's office symbol or the hull number of a ship. Each command or activity will determine makeup of the originator's code. Write the date in month-day-year order (e.g., 15 Mar 2024).
plain_language_rule: The sender's symbols block must include three elements in the upper left corner, stacked vertically: (1) SSIC code, (2) Originator's Code (either standalone or as part of a Serial Number), and (3) Date in month-day-year format. The originator's code is the office symbol of the drafter or the hull number for ships. Each command determines the specific makeup of their originator's code.
renderer_impact: Sender-symbol block positioning at top of page; affects LETTERHEAD to SSIC_DATE boundary spacing; determines vertical stacking order and alignment of SSIC, originator/serial, and date lines; interacts with C7-002 top margin requirements for letterhead paper
notes: CRITICAL FOR RENDERER GEOMETRY: This section directly affects header layout and top-of-page spacing. The sender-symbol block uses longest-line anchor alignment (all three lines share the same left starting position). Date format must be D Mon YY (e.g., 15 Mar 2024). Originator code handling is command-specific - renderer should not validate code content, only presence and positioning. When serial number is used (Ser X), it incorporates the originator code. Interaction with C7-002: on letterhead paper, typing starts more than 1 inch from top, so SSIC_DATE boundary must account for letterhead height plus this minimum spacing. AMBIGUITY: Manual states 'blocked one below the other' but does not specify exact spacing between the three lines; current implementation assumes 1 leading unit (14.4 pt) between each line per standard spacing model.
